1:
"God, who at sundry times and in divers manners spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets," The witnesses note the opening: God spoke of old in many ways by the prophets. On "God, who at sundry times and in divers manners spake in time past," the grammatical voices note the manner of the old revelation. Barnes and Robertson note the majestic opening (a finely-wrought Greek sentence): "God... spake (lalesas) at sundry times (polymeros — in many parts, by degrees, piecemeal) and in divers manners (polytropos — in many ways, by vision, dream, type, voice)" — the old revelation came fragmentarily and variously, not all at once.

On "unto the fathers by the prophets," the witnesses note the recipients and agents. Barnes and Calvin note that God spoke "unto the fathers (the patriarchs and people of Israel) by (in, through) the prophets" — the long line of inspired men through whom God revealed himself across the ages; the old covenant's mode of revelation.

The eastern Chrysostom notes the contrast prepared: the very manyness and variety of the old revelation, given piecemeal through many prophets, sets up the contrast with the one full and final word now spoken in the Son (v.2). The verse displays God speaking of old in many ways by the prophets.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the majestic opening — "God... spake at sundry times (in many parts, piecemeal) and in divers manners (in many ways)," the old revelation fragmentary and various (Barnes, Robertson); "unto the fathers (Israel) by (through) the prophets," the inspired line across the ages (Barnes, Calvin); the manyness setting up the contrast with the Son (Chrysostom). The verse displays the old revelation. The verse marks the speaker: God. The verse displays the manner: many parts, many ways. The verse displays the recipients: the fathers. The verse displays the agents: the prophets. The opening displays God's varied speech of old. The verse displays the speaking: in time past. The verse displays the variety: divers manners. The verse displays the prophets: the agents. God speaking of old in many ways by the prophets displays the majestic opening of the epistle: across the ages God revealed himself to the fathers fragmentarily and in many ways through the prophets — the old covenant's piecemeal revelation, now to be surpassed by the full word spoken in the Son. The verse displays the prophetic revelation, completed and crowned in the Son (v.2).
2:
"Hath in these last days spoken unto us by his Son, whom he hath appointed heir of all things, by whom also he made the worlds;" The witnesses note God's final word in his Son, heir and maker of all. On "in these last days spoken unto us by his Son," the grammatical voices note the climax. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with the old revelation: "in these last days (the close of the ages, the Messianic age) spoken unto us by (in) his Son" — over against the many prophets, God's final, complete word is spoken in one — his Son; the Son is himself the revelation.

On "whom he hath appointed heir of all things," the witnesses note the Son's dignity. Barnes and Calvin note the first of the Son's glories: "appointed heir (klerokon — lord and possessor by right) of all things" — the Son is constituted heir and possessor of the whole universe; all things are his by right.

On "by whom also he made the worlds," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the Son's creatorship: "by (through) whom also he made the worlds (tous aionas — the ages, the universe of time and space)" — the Son is the agent of creation; through him God made all things (cf. John 1:3; Colossians 1:16). The verse displays God's final word in his Son, heir and maker of all.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "in these last days (the Messianic age) spoken unto us by (in) his Son," God's final complete word in one, the Son himself the revelation (Barnes, Robertson); "appointed heir (lord and possessor) of all things," the Son heir of the universe by right (Barnes, Calvin); "by whom also he made the worlds (the ages, the universe)," the Son the agent of creation (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the Son's glory. The verse marks the word: by his Son. The verse displays the dignity: heir of all things. The verse displays the creatorship: made the worlds. The verse displays the climax: God's final word. God's final word in his Son, heir and maker of all, displays the surpassing of the old revelation: in these last days God has spoken not by many prophets but in his Son — whom he appointed heir of all things and through whom he made the worlds. The verse displays the Son's heirship and creatorship, expanded in his sevenfold glory (v.3).
3:
"Who being the brightness of his glory, and the express image of his person, and upholding all things by the word of his power, when he had by himself purged our sins, sat down on the right hand of the Majesty on high;" The witnesses note the Son's glory: radiance of God, upholder, purger of sins, enthroned. On "the brightness of his glory, and the express image of his person," the grammatical voices note the Son's deity. Barnes and Robertson note the two great terms: "the brightness (apaugasma — radiance, effulgence, the shining-forth) of his glory, and the express image (charakter — the exact impress, the stamp, as of a seal) of his person (hypostasis — his very substance, being)" — the Son is the radiance of God's glory and the exact imprint of his substance; true God of true God.

On "upholding all things by the word of his power," the witnesses note his sustaining of creation. Barnes and Calvin note that the Son is "upholding (pheron — bearing, carrying, sustaining) all things by the word of his power" — he not only made but sustains the universe, bearing it up by his powerful word; providence as well as creation is his.

On "when he had by himself purged our sins, sat down on the right hand of the Majesty," the eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the priestly work and exaltation: "when he had by himself (by his own sacrifice) purged (made purification for) our sins, sat down on the right hand of the Majesty on high" — having accomplished atonement by himself, he sat down enthroned at God's right hand; his finished priestly work crowned by his royal session. The verse displays the Son's glory: radiance, upholder, purger of sins, enthroned.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Son's deity — "the brightness (radiance) of his glory, and the express image (exact impress) of his person (substance)," radiance of God and exact imprint of his being (Barnes, Robertson); "upholding (sustaining) all things by the word of his power," bearing up the universe by his word (Barnes, Calvin); "when he had by himself purged our sins, sat down on the right hand of the Majesty," atonement accomplished and royal session attained (Chrysostom, Calvin). The verse displays the Son's sevenfold glory. The verse marks the radiance: of God's glory. The verse displays the image: exact impress. The verse displays the upholding: all things. The verse displays the priesthood: purged our sins. The glory displays the Son as God, sustainer, priest, and king. The verse displays the brightness: divine. The verse displays the upholding: providential. The verse displays the session: enthroned. The Son's glory — radiance of God, upholder of all, purger of sins, enthroned — displays the heart of the Christological prologue: the Son is the very radiance of God's glory and exact imprint of his substance, who upholds all things by his powerful word, and who, having made purification for sins by himself, sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high. The verse displays the Son's surpassing glory, better than the angels (v.4).
4:
"Being made so much better than the angels, as he hath by inheritance obtained a more excellent name than they." The brief verse notes the Son made better than the angels by his more excellent name. The grammatical voices note the superiority. Barnes and Robertson note the transition to the great argument of the chapter: "being made (genomenos — having become, in his exaltation) so much better (kreitton — superior, more excellent) than the angels" — the exalted Son is far superior to the angels; this superiority is now to be proved from Scripture.

On "made so much better," the witnesses note the sense. Barnes and Calvin note that this "being made better" refers not to his eternal nature (in which he was always superior) but to his exaltation as Mediator — in his enthronement he was shown to be, and exalted as, superior to the angels.

On "as he hath by inheritance obtained a more excellent name than they," the puritan Henry notes the ground: he is superior "as (in proportion as) he hath by inheritance obtained (inherited) a more excellent name (the name 'Son') than they" — the name "Son," belonging to him by inheritance, marks his superiority; the angels are servants, he the Son. The verse displays the Son made better than the angels by his more excellent name.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the transition — "being made (having become, in exaltation) so much better (superior) than the angels," the exalted Son far superior, now to be proved (Barnes, Robertson); "made better" referring to his exaltation as Mediator, not his eternal nature (Barnes, Calvin); "as he hath by inheritance obtained a more excellent name (Son) than they," the inherited name marking his superiority, the angels servants (Henry). The verse displays the superiority. The verse marks the comparison: better than angels. The verse displays the exaltation: made better. The verse displays the ground: a more excellent name. The verse displays the name: Son. The superiority displays the Son above the angels by his name. The verse displays the better: than angels. The verse displays the name: inherited. The verse displays the excellence: of the Son. The Son made better than the angels by his more excellent name displays the thesis of the chapter: the exalted Son is far superior to the angels — in proportion as he has by inheritance obtained a more excellent name than they, the name "Son," which the angels never receive. The verse displays the Son's superiority, proved from the Scriptures (vv.5-14).
5:
"For unto which of the angels said he at any time, Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee? And again, I will be to him a Father, and he shall be to me a Son?" The witnesses note the Son's unique name proved from Scripture — and the crux of the begetting. On "unto which of the angels said he... Thou art my Son," the grammatical voices note the argument. Barnes and Robertson note that the Son's superiority rests on the name never given to any single angel: "to which of the angels said he at any time, Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee?" (Psalm 2:7) and "I will be to him a Father, and he shall be to me a Son" (2 Samuel 7:14) — names of unique sonship given to the Messiah, never to an angel.

On "this day have I begotten thee," interpreters divide over the begetting. The grammatical-historical Barnes, with Clarke and Calvin, refer "this day have I begotten thee" to the resurrection and exaltation (Acts 13:33 applies it so; "the firstbegotten of the dead," Theodoret) — the day God publicly declared and constituted him Son; Calvin expressly rejects the eternal-generation reading here, taking the "today" as the resurrection. Others (Clarke notes) refer it to the incarnation. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley takes it of eternal generation ("I have begotten thee from eternity, one continued unsuccessive day"); Ellicott points to the "eternal Now," the day co-extensive with God's everlasting life.

The eastern Cyril notes the deity: the name "Son" given to the Messiah, never to angels, proves him more than a creature. The verse displays the Son's unique name proved from Scripture.

The witnesses agree the argument: the name "Son" — "Thou art my Son... I will be to him a Father" — is given to the Messiah and to no single angel, proving his superiority (Barnes, Robertson, Cyril). But "this day have I begotten thee" divides them: Barnes, Clarke, and Calvin refer it to the resurrection and exaltation (Acts 13:33; Calvin rejecting eternal generation here); Wesley and Ellicott take it of the eternal generation (the "eternal Now"); some refer it to the incarnation. The verse displays the begetting crux. The verse marks the name: my Son. The verse displays the argument: never said to an angel. The verse displays the debate: resurrection, eternity, or incarnation. The verse displays the deity: more than a creature. The name displays the Son's unique sonship above the angels. The verse displays the Son: begotten. The verse displays the Father: to him. The verse displays the angels: excluded. The Son's unique name proved from Scripture displays the first proof of his superiority and a famous crux: the name "Son" — "Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee" — is given to the Messiah, never to an angel, the begetting read as the resurrection and exaltation (Barnes, Clarke, Calvin) or the eternal generation (Wesley, Ellicott) or the incarnation. The verse displays the Son's name, before whom the angels worship (v.6).
6:
"And again, when he bringeth in the firstbegotten into the world, he saith, And let all the angels of God worship him." The witnesses note the angels commanded to worship the firstbegotten Son. On "when he bringeth in the firstbegotten into the world," the grammatical voices note the entrance. Barnes and Robertson note the further proof: "when he bringeth in (introduces) the firstbegotten (prototokon — the firstborn, supreme in rank and dignity) into the world" — God's bringing of his firstborn Son into the world; interpreters differ whether this refers to the incarnation (the first advent) or the second coming, but most take it of the Son's entrance into the world.

On "let all the angels of God worship him," the witnesses note the command. Barnes and Calvin note the citation (Deuteronomy 32:43 LXX / Psalm 97:7): "let all the angels of God worship (proskyneo — bow down, adore) him" — God commands the angels to worship the Son; the very beings to whom he is being compared are commanded to adore him, proving his infinite superiority.

On the worship, the eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the deity: if the angels are commanded to worship the Son, he is no creature but God; worship belongs to God alone, and the Son receives it. The verse displays the angels commanded to worship the firstbegotten Son.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the entrance — "when he bringeth in the firstbegotten (firstborn, supreme in rank) into the world," God introducing his Son (the incarnation or the second advent, Barnes, Robertson); "let all the angels of God worship (adore) him" (Deuteronomy 32:43 LXX), God commanding the angels to worship the Son (Barnes, Calvin); the worship proving his deity, for worship belongs to God alone (Chrysostom, Calvin). The verse displays the worship of the Son. The verse marks the entrance: the firstbegotten. The verse displays the command: angels worship him. The verse displays the deity: worship belongs to God. The verse displays the superiority: the worshipped above the worshippers. The command displays the angels adoring the Son. The verse displays the firstbegotten: brought in. The verse displays the worship: commanded. The verse displays the Son: adored. The angels commanded to worship the firstbegotten Son display a further proof of his supremacy: when God brings his firstborn into the world, he commands all the angels to worship him — the very beings to whom the Son is compared are bidden to adore him, proving his deity, for worship belongs to God alone. The verse displays the worshipped Son, over against the angels as servants (v.7).
7:
"And of the angels he saith, Who maketh his angels spirits, and his ministers a flame of fire." The brief verse notes the angels as servants, made winds and flame. The grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the citation (Psalm 104:4) showing the angels' subordinate, mutable nature: "of the angels he saith, Who maketh his angels spirits (pneumata — winds, or spirits), and his ministers a flame of fire" — the angels are God's servants, likened to winds and flames, swift and changeable instruments of his will.

On "spirits... a flame of fire," the witnesses note the imagery. Barnes and Calvin note that the angels are compared to "winds" and "fire" — created, ministering forces; whether "spirits" or "winds," the point is their service and mutability; they are made (created) instruments, not enthroned sons.

On the contrast, the eastern Cyril notes the comparison: over against the Son, who is worshipped and enthroned, the angels are mere servants, made by God to do his bidding; the contrast between the Son and the servants is the whole point. The verse displays the angels as servants, made winds and flame.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the citation (Psalm 104:4) — "Who maketh his angels spirits (winds), and his ministers a flame of fire," the angels God's servants, swift and changeable instruments (Barnes, Robertson); compared to winds and fire, created ministering forces, made instruments not enthroned sons (Barnes, Calvin); over against the worshipped Son, mere servants (Cyril). The verse displays the angel-servants. The verse marks the angels: made spirits. The verse displays the ministers: a flame of fire. The verse displays the nature: created, mutable. The verse displays the contrast: with the Son. The citation displays the angels as God's servants. The verse displays the spirits: winds. The verse displays the flame: fire. The verse displays the service: theirs. The angels as servants, made winds and flame, display the contrast that proves the Son's superiority: God makes his angels winds and his ministers a flame of fire — created, changeable servants who do his bidding — over against the Son, who is worshipped and enthroned as God. The verse displays the angel-servants, against whom the Son is addressed as God (v.8).
8:
"But unto the Son he saith, Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever: a sceptre of righteousness is the sceptre of thy kingdom." The witnesses note the Son addressed as God on an eternal throne — and the rendering crux. On "unto the Son he saith, Thy throne, O God," interpreters note the address. The grammatical-historical Barnes, with Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley, and Cyril, take the words (Psalm 45:6-7) as a direct address to the Son as God (the vocative ho theos): "Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever" — the Son is called God, his throne eternal; Barnes, Clarke, and Calvin defend this against the Unitarian rendering, arguing the whole scope, the Hebrew, and the ancient versions require the vocative. Robertson and Ellicott note the alternative rendering — "God is thy throne" (taking ho theos as nominative) — which the variant "his kingdom" might support; Robertson holds "either makes good sense," but Ellicott grants the vocative has "the support of almost all ancient authority."

On "is for ever and ever," the witnesses note the eternity. Barnes and Calvin note that the Son's throne "is for ever and ever" — his kingdom perpetual, eternal; an eternity that cannot be said of any creature, proving his deity, for whose throne can be established for ever but God's?

On "a sceptre of righteousness is the sceptre of thy kingdom," the grammatical-historical note the rule: "a sceptre of righteousness (a right, just sceptre) is the sceptre of thy kingdom" — the Son's reign is just and equitable; righteousness sways in his kingdom. The verse displays the Son addressed as God on an eternal throne.

The witnesses agree the substance: the words of Psalm 45 are applied to the Son, whose throne is eternal and whose sceptre is righteousness — an eternal, just dominion that proves him more than a creature (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). But the rendering divides them: the great majority (Gill, Barnes, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley, Cyril) take "Thy throne, O God" as a direct address to the Son as God (the vocative, a proof of his deity, defended against the Unitarian reading); Robertson and Ellicott note the alternative "God is thy throne" is grammatically possible (and suits the variant 'his kingdom'), though the vocative has nearly all ancient authority. The verse displays the deity crux. The verse marks the address: O God. The verse displays the debate: the Son called God, or God his throne. The verse displays the eternity: for ever and ever. The verse displays the rule: a sceptre of righteousness. The address displays the Son named God on an everlasting throne. The verse displays the throne: eternal. The verse displays the God: the Son. The verse displays the sceptre: righteous. The Son addressed as God on an eternal throne displays the climax of the proof and a deity crux: the Father says to the Son "Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever" — read by the overwhelming majority as a direct address calling the Son God (Gill, Barnes, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley), and by some as "God is thy throne" (Robertson, Ellicott allowing it grammatically) — his sceptre one of righteousness. The verse displays the Son's eternal throne, anointed above his fellows (v.9).
9:
"Thou hast loved righteousness, and hated iniquity; therefore God, even thy God, hath anointed thee with the oil of gladness above thy fellows." The witnesses note the Son anointed with gladness above his fellows for his love of righteousness. On "thou hast loved righteousness, and hated iniquity," the grammatical voices note the Son's moral perfection. Barnes and Robertson note the ground of his anointing: "thou hast loved righteousness, and hated iniquity" — the Son's perfect love of right and hatred of wrong; his moral excellence is the reason for his exaltation.

On "therefore God, even thy God, hath anointed thee with the oil of gladness," the witnesses note the anointing. Barnes and Calvin note that "therefore (because of this) God, even thy God, hath anointed (consecrated, exalted) thee with the oil of gladness" — God has anointed the Son with joy and honour; the "oil of gladness" the symbol of joyful exaltation and consecration.

On "above thy fellows," the eastern Cyril notes the anointing with the Spirit: the Son is anointed "above thy fellows (companions)" — beyond all others; Cyril notes Christ was anointed not with material oil but with the Holy Ghost (the oil of gladness), the source of spiritual joy, above all his fellows. The verse displays the Son anointed with gladness above his fellows.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ground — "thou hast loved righteousness, and hated iniquity," the Son's perfect moral excellence the reason for his exaltation (Barnes, Robertson); "therefore God, even thy God, hath anointed thee with the oil of gladness," God consecrating and exalting him with joy and honour (Barnes, Calvin); "above thy fellows," beyond all others, anointed with the Holy Ghost the oil of gladness (Cyril). The verse displays the anointing. The verse marks the love: of righteousness. The verse displays the anointing: oil of gladness. The verse displays the source: God thy God. The verse displays the measure: above thy fellows. The anointing displays the Son exalted for his righteousness. The verse displays the righteousness: loved. The verse displays the gladness: anointed. The verse displays the fellows: surpassed. The Son anointed with gladness above his fellows for his love of righteousness displays the reward of his moral perfection: because he loved righteousness and hated iniquity, God anointed him with the oil of gladness above all his fellows — exalting him with joy and honour. The verse displays the anointed Son, addressed also as the eternal Creator (vv.10-12).
10:
"And, Thou, Lord, in the beginning hast laid the foundation of the earth; and the heavens are the works of thine hands:" The witnesses note the Son addressed as the eternal Creator. On "Thou, Lord, in the beginning hast laid the foundation of the earth," the grammatical voices note the creatorship. Barnes and Robertson note the further citation (Psalm 102:25-27), applied to the Son: "Thou, Lord, in the beginning hast laid the foundation of the earth" — the Son is addressed as "Lord" and as the Creator who laid the earth's foundation in the beginning; words spoken of Jehovah in the Psalm are here applied to the Son.

On "the heavens are the works of thine hands," the witnesses note the heavens' authorship. Barnes and Calvin note that "the heavens are the works of thine hands" — the Son made not only the earth but the heavens; the whole creation is his handiwork; a striking ascription of full creatorship to the Son.

On the application, the eastern Cyril and Reformed note the deity: that the writer applies to the Son words addressed in the Psalm to Jehovah the Creator is a decisive proof of the Son's true deity — he is the eternal Lord who made heaven and earth. The verse displays the Son addressed as the eternal Creator.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the citation (Psalm 102:25-27) applied to the Son — "Thou, Lord, in the beginning hast laid the foundation of the earth," the Son addressed as Lord and Creator (Barnes, Robertson); "the heavens are the works of thine hands," the Son maker of heaven and earth, the whole creation his (Barnes, Calvin); the application of words said of Jehovah to the Son proving his deity (Cyril, Reformed). The verse displays the Son the Creator. The verse marks the title: Lord. The verse displays the work: laid the earth's foundation. The verse displays the heavens: his handiwork. The verse displays the deity: words of Jehovah applied to him. The citation displays the Son as the eternal maker of all. The verse displays the Lord: the Son. The verse displays the earth: founded. The verse displays the heavens: made. The Son addressed as the eternal Creator displays the height of the proof: the Father applies to the Son words spoken of Jehovah — "Thou, Lord, in the beginning hast laid the foundation of the earth, and the heavens are the works of thine hands" — ascribing to him the full work of creation, a decisive witness to his deity. The verse displays the Son's creatorship, set against the perishing creation (vv.11-12).
11:
"They shall perish; but thou remainest; and they all shall wax old as doth a garment;" The brief verse notes the creation perishing while the Son remains unchanged. The grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast between the creation and the Creator: "they (the heavens and earth) shall perish (pass away, be dissolved); but thou remainest (continuest, abidest)" — the created universe is perishable, but the Son endures; the contrast of the changing creation and the unchanging Creator.

On "they all shall wax old as doth a garment," the witnesses note the figure. Barnes and Calvin note the image: "they all shall wax old (grow old, decay) as doth a garment" — the heavens and earth, like a worn-out garment, will grow old and decay; the whole creation ages and wears out.

On the contrast, the eastern Cyril notes the deity: the perishing of the creation over against the abiding of the Son proves his eternity and immutability; he who outlasts and outlives the universe he made is God, not a creature. The verse displays the creation perishing while the Son remains unchanged.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "they (the universe) shall perish (pass away); but thou remainest (abidest)," the creation perishable but the Son enduring (Barnes, Robertson); "they all shall wax old (decay) as doth a garment," the heavens aging like a worn-out garment (Barnes, Calvin); the contrast proving the Son's eternity and immutability, he who outlasts creation being God (Cyril). The verse displays the perishing creation. The verse marks the perishing: they shall perish. The verse displays the abiding: thou remainest. The verse displays the figure: wax old as a garment. The verse displays the deity: the Son eternal. The contrast displays the Son enduring while creation decays. The verse displays the creation: perishing. The verse displays the Son: remaining. The verse displays the garment: worn out. The creation perishing while the Son remains unchanged displays the proof of his eternity: the heavens and earth will perish and grow old like a worn-out garment, but the Son remains — the unchanging Creator who outlasts his perishing creation, and so is God. The verse displays the Son's permanence, the same for ever (v.12).
12:
"And as a vesture shalt thou fold them up, and they shall be changed: but thou art the same, and thy years shall not fail." The witnesses note the Son the same for ever while the heavens are changed. On "as a vesture shalt thou fold them up, and they shall be changed," the grammatical voices note the figure completed. Barnes and Robertson note the image: "as a vesture (garment, mantle) shalt thou fold them up (roll them up, as one rolls up a worn cloak), and they shall be changed (transformed, made new)" — the Son will fold up the heavens like a worn garment and change them; the creation is mutable in his hands.

On "but thou art the same," the witnesses note the immutability. Barnes and Calvin note the great contrast: "but thou art the same (ho autos — the unchanging One)" — over against the changing creation, the Son is ever the same, immutable; the unchangeableness of God ascribed to the Son.

On "thy years shall not fail," the eastern Cyril notes the eternity: "thy years shall not fail (not come to an end, not be exhausted)" — the Son's existence is without end; his years are eternal; the very attributes of God — immutability and eternity — belong to the Son. The verse displays the Son the same for ever while the heavens are changed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the figure completed — "as a vesture shalt thou fold them up (roll them up like a worn cloak), and they shall be changed," the Son folding up and changing the heavens (Barnes, Robertson); "but thou art the same (the unchanging One)," the immutability of God ascribed to the Son (Barnes, Calvin); "thy years shall not fail," his existence eternal, without end (Cyril). The verse displays the Son's immutability. The verse marks the figure: fold them up. The verse displays the change: they shall be changed. The verse displays the immutability: thou art the same. The verse displays the eternity: thy years fail not. The contrast displays the Son unchanging and eternal. The verse displays the vesture: folded. The verse displays the same: the Son. The verse displays the years: unfailing. The Son the same for ever while the heavens are changed displays the climax of the proof of his deity: he will fold up the heavens like a worn garment and change them, but he himself is ever the same, his years never failing — the immutability and eternity of God belonging to the Son. The verse displays the unchanging Son, enthroned at God's right hand (v.13).
13:
"But to which of the angels said he at any time, Sit on my right hand, until I make thine enemies thy footstool?" The witnesses note the Son enthroned at God's right hand, never an angel. On "to which of the angels said he at any time, Sit on my right hand," the grammatical voices note the final proof. Barnes and Robertson note the closing citation (Psalm 110:1): "to which of the angels said he at any time, Sit on my right hand?" — the place of supreme honour and authority, at God's right hand, is given to the Son, never to any angel; the rhetorical question expects the answer "none."

On "until I make thine enemies thy footstool," the witnesses note the subjugation. Barnes and Calvin note the promise: "until I make thine enemies thy footstool (a stool for thy feet, the figure of complete subjugation)" — all the Son's enemies will be subdued under his feet; his reign continues until total victory; the figure of a conqueror placing his foot on the necks of the vanquished.

On the contrast, the eastern Cyril notes the exaltation: the Son sits enthroned at God's right hand, ruling until all foes are subdued — an honour and dominion never given to any angel, who are but servants. The verse displays the Son enthroned at God's right hand.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the final proof (Psalm 110:1) — "to which of the angels said he, Sit on my right hand?", the place of supreme honour given to the Son never an angel (Barnes, Robertson); "until I make thine enemies thy footstool," all his foes subdued under his feet, his reign till total victory (Barnes, Calvin); the Son enthroned, ruling till all are subdued, an honour never given an angel (Cyril). The verse displays the enthroned Son. The verse marks the seat: my right hand. The verse displays the question: never an angel. The verse displays the subjugation: enemies a footstool. The verse displays the contrast: with the servant-angels. The citation displays the Son enthroned above all. The verse displays the sitting: at God's right hand. The verse displays the enemies: subdued. The verse displays the angels: excluded. The Son enthroned at God's right hand, never an angel, displays the closing proof of his supremacy: to no angel did God ever say "Sit on my right hand until I make thine enemies thy footstool" — the place of supreme honour and the promise of total victory belong to the Son alone. The verse displays the enthroned Son, served by the ministering angels (v.14).
14:
"Are they not all ministering spirits, sent forth to minister for them who shall be heirs of salvation?" The witnesses note the angels as ministering spirits serving the heirs of salvation. On "are they not all ministering spirits," the grammatical voices note the angels' office. Barnes and Robertson note the summary of the angels' true rank: "are they not all ministering spirits (leitourgika pneumata — serving spirits, spirits employed in service)?" — the rhetorical question affirms that the angels, all of them, are servants; their office is ministry, not dominion.

On "sent forth to minister," the witnesses note their mission. Barnes and Calvin note that they are "sent forth (dispatched, commissioned) to minister (diakonian — to render service)" — the angels are sent out by God on errands of service; they are God's messengers and helpers, not enthroned sons.

On "for them who shall be heirs of salvation," the puritan Henry notes the objects: their service is "for them who shall be heirs of salvation (the redeemed, those who will inherit salvation)" — the angels minister to and for believers; the exalted Son is served, the angels serve, and they serve even the saints. The verse displays the angels as ministering spirits serving the heirs of salvation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the angels' true rank — "are they not all ministering spirits (serving spirits)?", all the angels servants, their office ministry not dominion (Barnes, Robertson); "sent forth (commissioned) to minister (serve)," dispatched on errands of service, God's messengers (Barnes, Calvin); "for them who shall be heirs of salvation," ministering to and for believers, the redeemed (Henry). The verse displays the ministering angels. The verse marks the office: ministering spirits. The verse displays the mission: sent forth to serve. The verse displays the objects: the heirs of salvation. The verse displays the contrast: servants, not the enthroned Son. The summary displays the angels serving the saints. The verse displays the spirits: ministering. The verse displays the sending: forth. The verse displays the heirs: served. The angels as ministering spirits serving the heirs of salvation display the conclusion of the chapter's argument: all the angels are but servants, sent forth to minister for those who will inherit salvation — servants of the saints, over against the Son who is enthroned and worshipped as God. The verse displays the serving angels, grounding the warning not to neglect so great salvation (2:1-4).
